title: Reading Files 📖
description: Learn to safely open and read data from standard text files.

slides.title: Data Persistence 💾
slides.content:
Right now, your Ruby scripts lose all their memory and variables the second the script naturally finishes running.

To safely permanently save data, you must write it out to your physical hard drive as a File (File I/O = Input/Output).

slides.title: The File Class 📂
slides.content:
Ruby has a famously powerful built-in `File` class completely dedicated to seamlessly interacting with your hard drive.

To open any file, you call `File.open("filename.txt", "mode")`.

The Mode dictates what you plan to aggressively do with it (`"r"` for reading, `"w"` for writing).

slides.title: Reading Data
slides.content: Imagine you have a text file filled with cool data gracefully sitting smoothly next to your script.
slides.codeSnippet:
# 1. Open safely in Read mode
my_file = File.open("data.txt", "r")

# 2. Extract every single line into a massive string array
puts my_file.read

# 3. Always strictly manually close the file when finished!
my_file.close

quizData.question: Which specific mode parameter strictly firmly tells Ruby you ONLY want perfectly safe read access to the file?
quizData.explanation: Standard "r" stands specifically for 'read-only', preventing you from accidentally beautifully deleting your data.

slides.title: The Magic Block 🧱
slides.content:
Manually remembering to securely definitively run `my_file.close` is tedious and extremely prone to dangerous memory leaks if your script crashes.

Ruby solves this easily! If you pass a Block heavily containing your code right into `File.open`, Ruby automatically smartly closes the file the exact second the block finishes executing!

slides.title: Safe File Opening
slides.content: Notice how we absolutely no longer need a `close` command! Ruby gracefully precisely manages the cleanup flawlessly.
slides.codeSnippet:
File.open("secrets.txt", "r") do |file|
  puts "File is open!"
  puts file.read
end

puts "File instantly closed securely here!"

quizData.question: Why is utilizing a `do...end` Block the universally preferred way to open and perfectly manipulate files?

options.text: It instantly flawlessly automatically ensures the file reliably fully closes afterward, protecting logic memory.

quizData.explanation: Safety first! The extremely handy Block completely eliminates the risk of human-error memory leak bugs.
